summary: Simon Späti argues that data modeling is the overlooked foundation of data engineering that deserves renewed attention. He traces the history from Codd's relational model through Kimball and Inmon's data warehousing approaches to modern techniques. The post explains why data modeling fell out of favor—rushed MVPs, small teams under pressure, lack of time—and why it's resurging as organizations realize that solid data architecture determines the success of data-driven culture. Späti introduces key concepts including normalization, metrics/KPIs, slowly changing dimensions, and ERDs as foundational knowledge for data engineers.
key_points: Data modeling creates a structured representation of organizational data that serves as a blueprint for business value, yet most projects initially ignore it due to time constraints and fast-paced development
The history of data modeling spans from 1960s hierarchical models through Codd's relational revolution, Kimball/Inmon's data warehousing approaches, to modern techniques like data vault and data mesh
Organizations often build 'quick and dirty' MVPs under pressure, then patch them for production instead of stepping back to build proper foundations
Data complexity grows exponentially without proper modeling—similar to Big-O notation, data copying can reach O(N²) if you don't manage how data flows through the organization
The resurgence of data modeling interest comes from organizations realizing that solid data architecture and tool choices directly impact data-driven culture success and costs
money_quotes: Most data projects initially ignore data modeling because of time constraints, fast-paced development, or sometimes lack of knowledge.

Instead of investing time into sketching out how the data should flow from its source, the organization's requirements, to how the end user wants to analyze its data, projects get built quickly and with a 'dirty' MVP.
Due to a lack of time and resources, these small teams get pressured to deliver a valuable product to the business immediately after showing a working MVP.
If you do not know about Big-O notation, you are probably not delivering on good performant code.
If we see data engineering through the lens of data modeling, I am optimistic that we have an angle to work against these forces and keep the complexity of storing data low.
People have realized that a solid data architecture and choice of tools matter a lot regarding the success of a data-driven culture and the cost of using data.
Instead of thinking in your small garden ahead, you zoom out and see the whole field.
key_insight: Data modeling is experiencing a well-deserved resurgence because organizations have learned that skipping foundational data architecture in favor of quick MVPs leads to exponentially growing complexity and costs.
